details.text: Derivative: يَتَسَاءَلُونَ is from the verb تَسَاءَلَ. It means some of them are asking others, or they are asking one another.
Derivative: لِبَاسًا means as a garment or an item of clothing that covers. That is as the night which it is describing covers everything with its darkness.
Derivative: مَعَاشًا can mean both a means of livelihood and a time of livelihood.
Derivative: شِدَادًا refers to powerful constructions which is in reference to the seven heavens.
Derivative: سِرَاجًا refers to anything that emits light or is lit, like a lamp. Here it is being used to refer to the sun.

Derivative: وَهَّاجًا comes from وَهَجَ – يَهِجُ – وَهْجًا and is in the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah - hyperbolised) form of فَعَّال (Fa’’aal). وَهَجَ (Wahaja) means to be brilliant and blazing. وَهَّاجًا (Wahhaajah) which is in the hyperbolised and emphasised or more powerful form, therefore means “an incredibly brilliant blazing (سِرَاجًا - lamp or sun)”.

Derivative: الْمُعْصِرَاتِ comes from أَعْصَرَ – إِعْصَار which can mean to squeezed out or to capacity. الْمُعْصِرَاتِ can refer to the clouds which are squeezed and are on the verge of raining due to them being full. Or it can refer to the winds that stir and agitate the clouds and squeeze them together.
Derivative: ثَجَّاجًا – ثّجَّ refers to consecutive pouring or to heavy and abundant pouring.
Derivative: حَبًّا is used to refer to all types of grain and crops.
Derivative: نَبَاتًا is used to refer to all types of green vegetation and plants.
Derivative: أَلْفَافًا could be the plural of لَفِيفٌ (Lafeef) which means wrapped around or gathered together. أَلْفَافًا means that the gardens are intertwined with each other.
Derivative: جَنَّاتٍ refers to lush gardens.
Derivative: مِيقَاتًا refers to a fixed and set appointed time.

Derivative: الْفَصْلِ – فَصْل is when two things or matters are separated to the extent that they are clearly apart and distinct from each other. The day (يَوْمَ) of الْفَصْلِ refers to the Day of Judgement when many separations will take place like between the truth and falsehood and between believers and disbelievers, between those who were followed and those who followed them etc.

Derivative: تَأْتُونَ is from أَتَى – يَأْتِي. The verb أَتَى in Arabic does not just mean to come like جَاءَ but can also mean to come in submission like in Surah Fussilat verse 11. تَأْتُونَ “You will come” therefore implies that they will give themselves up in submission without choice, which is in contrast to the choice they had to rebel during the life of this world.

Derivative: النَّبَإِ means news. النَّبَإِ (Naba’) refers to a type of news which is greater and more important than خَبَر (Khabar) which is also a word to refer to news. النَّبَإِ (Naba’) is therefore used for major news. النَّبَإِ (Naba’) also implies that there should be a reaction or some action undertaken in relation to it and is not just news that is heard and taken in without consequence. It also refers to a tangible news or event that has occurred.

Derivative: سُيِّرَتِ is in the مَبْنِي علَى المَجْهُول form (i.e., passive voice) from سَيَّرَ – تَسْيِير which means to be made to move with ease and without great effort from where they were. It can mean to be removed and gone away with i.e., made to disappear.

Derivative: سَرَابًا means a mirage and here it implies that it will become like nothing, just like a mirage turns out to be nothing. Or it could refer to the impression that is formed when the sight is seen by the human eye in the case where it is believed that what they are seeing cannot be real and must just be a mirage.

Derivative: مِرْصَادًا means to be waiting in ambush. It refers to a place that is ideal to lie in wait in to undertake an ambush. Here Jahannam (hell) is being described as a place that has been lying in wait to undertake its attack.

Derivative: مَآبًا refers in Arabic to a place that you keep going or returning back to. Here it means the place of return i.e., that will be an abode and home. مَآبًا is a Masdar (مَصْدَر) and is also a Zharf Makaan (ظَرْف مَكان) and as such it can mean “stay” or “staying” itself or it can mean a place to stay.

Derivative: الطَّاغِينَ – It is from طُغْيان which means rebellion in the sense of doing whatever one wishes whilst infringing upon the rights of the higher authority or the rights of others. الطَّاغِينَ are the rebellious and transgressive.

Derivative: أَحْقَابًا refers to a quantity of time. It is the plural of حُقْب (Huqb) and refers to a long period of time. There are differences in opinion regarding how long it is, but as an example it has been said to be equivalent to eighty years, every day of which is a thousand years. The use of the plural of أَحْقَابًا has been said to mean one حُقْب (Huqb) after another, in a continuous and endless manner.
Derivative: لَابِثِينَ comes from لَبِثَ – يَلْبَثُ which means to stay in a place for a long time. لَابِثِينَ means “They will be staying for a very long time”.
Derivative: حَمِيمًا refers to scorching scalding water.
Derivative: غَسَّاقًا refers to infected pus or blood i.e., filthy and putrid excretions from the human body.

Derivative: وِفَاقًا is the Masdar of وَافَقَ which means to be agreeable and consistent (with something). The use of the Masdar وِفَاقًا here implies that it is the most appropriate and befitting i.e., exact in respect to what is deserved.
Derivative: جَزَاءً means recompense and payback.

Derivative: أَحْصَيْنَا “We have enumerated” from أَحْصَى – إِحْصاء. It is used to mean to count however there is a difference between simple counting and إِحْصاء which means to count, enumerate but also to record the count to make sure it is protected and preserved i.e., it is a more thorough process than just counting.

Derivative: مُخْتَلِفُونَ means to be differing (about something) i.e., holding different views or opinions and to be in some discord. The use of the noun instead of the verb indicates to a permanent state rather than something that just occurred in which case the verb would be used. This indicated that the “differing” was something that reflected a constant and continuous state.

Derivative: مَفَازًا can be a Masdar (مَصْدَر – root noun of the verb), in which case it means “success”, or it can be a Zharf Al-Makaan (ظَرْفُ المَكان - Adverb of place), in which case it means the place or abode of success, or it can means a Zharf Az-Zamaan (ظَرْفُ الزَّمَان - Adverb of time), in which case it would mean the time of success.
Derivative: مُتَّقِينَ means “the people of Taqwa (تَقْوَى)”. Taqwa (تَقْوَى) refers to the taking of precautions due to fear or to protect oneself or things.
Derivative: حَدَائِقَ is the plural of حَدِيقَة (Hadeeqah) which is a garden with a high fence around it. It also carries the implication of being surrounded by beauty.
Derivative: أَتْرَابًا means equal in age i.e., the most compatible (of companions).
Derivative: كَوَاعِبَ is the plural of كَاعِب which refers to a young woman who is in the best physical shape or prime.
Derivative: كَأْسًا is translated as a “cup” however it was only used in classical Arabic to describe the cup which had been filled to its brim.
Derivative: دِهَاقًا means full to the point that it overflows or splashes.
Derivative: لَغْوًا refers to useless, vain and idle talk.
Derivative: كِذَّابًا means to lie against someone.

Derivative: حِسَابًا here refers to when someone is given so much that he says: “I don’t need anymore” or “That’s enough for me, I can’t receive anymore”. حِسَابًا therefore means: “To the point where somebody says: “No more”.
Derivative: عَطَاءً is something that is given freely like a gift or grant.
Derivative: الرَّحْمَٰنِ – The exceedingly merciful.
Derivative: صَوَابًا means true and it refers to speech that is correct and to the point with the implication in this context of brevity,
Derivative: مَآبًا means a place of return.
Derivative: اتَّخَذَ does not just mean to take (something) but rather to take hold of something.

- This can be understood in accordance with the rules of grammar in three ways: “That is the true day”, “That day is the truth” and “Know, that this is that day of truth” in the case where in the third understanding there are words which have been omitted in the text but understood implicitly. The majority held view is the second: “That day (ذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمُ) is the ultimate truth (الْحَقُّ)”. Normally the خَبَر (predicate) is in the Nakirah form without ال (Alif Laam) and when it is used it indicates to exclusivity (الحَصْر) i.e., it is the truth like no other.

Derivative: كَلَّا some classical grammarians believed that this statement meant: “Stop” as a form of deterrent, in the context of whatever you are talking about, just “stop”, the matter isn’t like you say or think, rather, I will tell you how it really is or will be.

كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ

- “كَلَّا (Kallaa) you will soon come to know” – The كَلَّا (Kallaa) here can have two meanings depending on if there is a pause between it and سَيَعْلَمُونَ (they will come to know). If there is a pause, then its meaning is “They should stop this nonsense and these different opinions they are presenting” followed by “and they are all about to find out”. If, however, the two statements are said together without a pause, then its meaning would be in line with the meaning of حَقًّا (Haqqan) which means truly i.e., “Truly they are going to find out soon” or like “No, no (or nay) … Soon they are going to find out” in the case where the “no” implies “truly”.
Derivative: قَدَّمَتْ in this context means to have sent forward or ahead.

- English is incapable of encapsulating the exact meaning of يَا لَيْتَنِي. It depicts a situation where a person is so scared, overwhelmed and feeling so hopeless that he doesn’t have any words to express himself i.e., speechless and dumbfound. It can be translated as “If only …” with the emotions of despair, regret, hopelessness, fear and being overwhelmed attached to it.
Nouman Ali Khan Notes

ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ

- The repetition in this verse from the previous verse of كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ “Nay, they will soon come to know” has been said to be for the purpose of مُبَالغَة (emphasis or hyperbole). Another view is that each statement is related to a different event that they will come to known about i.e., two separate events. One is about the day of resurrection and the second is about hellfire i.e., these are the two events or realities that they will soon really get to know.

- The سَ attached to the beginning of the present tense verb which can be translated as “will i.e., happen in the future) implies that it will be soon: “You will “soon” come to know”. The سَ which is the short version of سَوْفَ and implies that whatever is coming is very close. Allah is making sure that it is understood to be close by the use of the سِين (Seen – سَ) here and also by stating in the final verse of this Surah: إِنَّا أَنْذَرْنَاكُمْ عَذَابًا قَرِيبًا “No doubt, We are warning you a punishment that is close”.

Derivative: مِهَادًا means a smooth plain. A word related to مِهَادًا (Mihaad) is مَهْدٌ (Mahd) which means a cradle which signifies a place that is comfortable. As such the smoothness of مِهَادًا is connected to the meaning of comfort. مِهَادًا (Mihaad) is also used in Arabic to refer to a bed or place that you find rest in. These three meanings of smoothness, comfort and rest can be connected to the meaning of مِهادًا (Mihaad) in respect to the earth being made as a place suitable for humans settling upon it.

Derivative: أَوْتَادًا means pegs like what are used to hold down tents firmly. Tents are also called أَوْتَادًا in accordance with the custom in Arabic of calling something by the name of its most important or prominent feature.

Derivative: سُبَاتًا literally means: As something that cuts off. Sleep cuts your body off from your soul, from daily activities, from your family and other people. When you are in deep sleep you are cut off from everything in life. It has been interpreted to imply a rest for us and our bodies by cutting off all that keeps us busy and preoccupied.
Derivative: نَوْمَ (Nawm) refers to sleep that is deep.
